title: Pot Odds & Expected Value
subtitle: When a call is mathematically justified
intro: Pot odds compare the size of the pot to the price of a call. If you must call $10 into a $40 pot, you are getting 4:1 — you need to win more than one time in five (20% equity) to break even on that call.
Expected value (EV) extends this idea: multiply each outcome by its probability and sum. A +EV call loses often in the short run but profits over thousands of similar spots.
Implied odds adjust for future chips you may win when you hit your draw. This lesson connects raw math to Hold'em decisions — flush draws, gutshots, and river calls.

Pot odds and minimum defense frequency (MDF) are two sides of the same coin: pot odds tell the caller what equity they need; MDF tells the defender how often they must continue so a bluff cannot auto-profit. On the river, a half-pot bet requires roughly 25% equity to call but also implies the defender should continue about 67% of the time to prevent pure bluffs from printing money. GTO players balance value and bluffs at each sizing; exploitative players attack when opponents fold too much or call too light. The math is simple — the discipline is applying it under pressure.

primer.heading: Pot odds: the price to continue
body: **Pot odds** compare what you must call to what you can win. Call `$10` into a `$40` pot (the pot after the bet) and you are getting **4-to-1** — you risk one to win four.
Convert that ratio to a percentage so you can compare it against your chance of winning. The **breakeven equity** is `call / (call + total pot)`.

keyTerms.definition: The ratio of the call price to the total pot you stand to win.
The minimum win probability that makes a call exactly break even in the long run.

visual.caption: Call when your hand's equity exceeds this percentage.

example.prompt: Villain bets `$20` into an `$80` pot. You must call `$20`. What equity do you need?
steps: The pot you can win is `$80 + $20 = $100`; you call `$20`.
example.result: You need ≥ ~17% equity to call — the pot lays you 5-to-1.

primer.heading: Counting outs (rules of 2 and 4)
body: An **out** is a card that completes your hand. A flush draw has **9 outs** (13 of a suit minus the 4 you can already see).
Shortcut: on the flop, multiply outs by **4** for your chance to hit by the river; with one card to come, multiply by **2**. Nine outs ≈ 36% by the river, ≈ 18% on the turn.

keyTerms.definition: A remaining card that improves your hand to a likely winner.
Your share of the pot — the probability your hand wins by showdown.

visual.caption: Common draws via the rule of 4 (flop to river)

primer.heading: The decision: equity vs breakeven
body: Here is the whole game: if your **equity** beats the **breakeven** number, call; if not, fold. Pot odds give the threshold, outs give your equity.
Pair them up: a flush draw (≈36%) against a half-pot bet that needs ~25% to call is an easy continue.

example.prompt: Pot `$100`, villain bets `$100` (pot-sized). You hold a flush draw on the flop. Call?
steps: Flush draw equity ≈ 36% by the river (9 outs × 4).
example.result: Ahead of the price using the rule of 4; with implied odds it is a clear call.

primer.heading: Expected value
body: **Expected value (EV)** sums each outcome times its probability. A call can lose most individual times yet still be +EV across thousands of similar spots.
EV of a call = `equity × (pot won) − (1 − equity) × (call)`. When that number is positive, the call makes money long-term.

keyTerms.definition: The probability-weighted average result of a decision over the long run.

visual.caption: Positive EV means the play profits if repeated.

example.prompt: You call `$10` to win the `$30` already in the pot, holding 40% equity. EV?

primer.heading: Implied and reverse implied odds
body: **Implied odds** count chips you expect to win on **later** streets when you hit. They justify calls that raw pot odds reject — like **set-mining** a small pair, which flops a set only ~12% but can win a whole stack.
**Reverse implied odds** are the mirror: hands that win small and lose big, like a non-nut flush that pays off the nut flush. Deep stacks magnify both effects.

keyTerms.definition: Extra chips you can win on future streets if you complete your hand.
The risk of losing additional chips when you make a strong-looking but second-best hand.

visual.title: Set-mining
visual.text: A pocket pair flops a set ≈12% (about 1 in 8). It needs deep stacks so the rare hit pays for the many misses.

primer.heading: MDF: the defender's mirror
body: Pot odds tell the **caller** what equity they need. **Minimum defense frequency (MDF)** tells the **defender** how often to continue so a pure bluff cannot auto-profit.
MDF = `pot / (pot + bet)`. Fold more often than this against a pure bluffer and they print money betting any two cards.

keyTerms.definition: The smallest fraction of your range you must continue with to stop bluffs from being automatically profitable.

example.prompt: River pot is `$100`. Villain bets `$50` (half pot). What is the MDF?
example.result: Continue with ≈67% of your range to deny auto-profit bluffs.

primerNarration.title: The price to continue
primerNarration.caption: Pot odds compare the chips you must call to the total you can win. Calling ten into a forty-dollar pot gives you four to one. Turn that ratio into a percentage — your breakeven equity is the call divided by the pot plus the call.

primerNarration.title: Counting your outs
primerNarration.caption: An out is a card that completes your hand. A flush draw has nine outs. On the flop, multiply outs by four for your chance to get there by the river; with one card left, multiply by two. Nine outs is about thirty-six percent by the river.

primerNarration.title: Equity versus breakeven
primerNarration.caption: Here is the whole game: if your equity beats the breakeven number, call; if it does not, fold. A flush draw is about thirty-six percent, so against a half-pot bet that needs twenty-five percent, you call.

primerNarration.title: EV over the long run
primerNarration.caption: Expected value sums each outcome times its probability. A call can lose most of the time and still make money over thousands of spots. Multiply your equity by the pot, subtract your call times the chance you miss, and if that is positive, it profits.

primerNarration.title: Chips you win later
primerNarration.caption: Implied odds count the chips you expect to win on later streets when you hit. They justify set-mining a small pair, which flops a set only about one in eight times but can win a whole stack. Reverse implied odds are the opposite — hands that win small and lose big.

primerNarration.title: The defender's mirror
primerNarration.caption: Pot odds tell the caller what equity they need. Minimum defense frequency tells the defender how often to keep going so a pure bluff cannot auto-profit. It is the pot divided by the pot plus the bet — about sixty-seven percent against a half-pot bet.

explanations.A: $80 is pot before the bet only.

questions.question: Pot $100, bet $100, you have 30% equity. Calling is:

explanations.A: Correct — need ~33% at 1:1 pot odds; 30% is short.
Correct — 1:1 means win half the time.

remediation.question: If equity < pot-odds breakeven, call is:

explanations.A: Correct — math says fold unless implied odds help.

questions.question: Implied odds matter most when:
options: You may win a large stack if you hit your draw
Pot is tiny and stacks shallow

explanations.A: Correct — future bets inflate reward beyond current pot.
explanations.C: Nuts don't need implied odds for a draw.
explanations.D: Paired board is a separate concern.

explanations.A: Correct — more chips to win when you hit.

remediation.question: Set mining preflop needs:
options: Implied odds — flop a set rarely but win big

explanations.A: Correct — ~12% flop set; profit from stacking opponent.
explanations.C: Pocket pair preflop equity is low.

explanations.A: Correct — positive expected value over many trials.
explanations.D: Pot odds help compute EV but differ.

remediation.question: Short-term results:
options: Can diverge from EV due to variance

questions.question: Flush draw plus open-ended straight draw has more equity than flush alone because:

explanations.A: Correct — combined outs (minus overlap) boost equity.

questions.question: Reverse implied odds hurt when:
options: Hitting your hand may lose a big pot (second best)

explanations.A: Correct — dominated draws like non-nut flush can lose stacks.
explanations.B: RIO is about losing big when you hit.
explanations.C: Small pot limits loss, not RIO definition.

remediation.question: Non-nut flush on paired board risks:

explanations.A: Correct — paired board full houses possible.
explanations.D: Flush beats high card but loses to boat.

remediation.question: Second-nut flush draw has:
options: Reverse implied odds vs nut flush holder

explanations.A: Correct — making flush may still lose.

questions.question: Pot $200, villain shoves $100. You need 25% equity (call $100 to win $400). Villain's range is 30% bluffs you beat, 70% value you lose to. Call?

explanations.A: Correct — 30% equity exceeds 25% breakeven; bluff-catching is +EV.
Correct — need enough bluffs in range.

remediation.question: Polarized river range often contains:
options: Strong value or bluffs — fewer medium hands

questions.question: Pot $100, bet $50. Minimum defense frequency (MDF) to prevent auto-profit bluffs?

remediation.question: MDF tells you:
options: How often to continue vs bet to deny bluff profit

explanations.A: Correct — balance defense vs pot odds.

remediation.question: Folding more than MDF vs pure bluffs:

questions.question: River pot is $120. Villain bets $60 (half pot). MDF — the minimum fraction of your range you must continue with to prevent bluffs from auto-profiting — is approximately:

explanations.D: 25% is the breakeven equity needed to call, not MDF.

questions.question: $1/$2, 100bb stacks. UTG opens $6, you call on the BTN with 5♠ 5♥ (set mine). You need implied odds because:
options: You flop a set ~12% of the time but can win a large pot when you do
Small pairs always win without hitting
Pot odds alone justify any call preflop

explanations.A: Correct — raw preflop equity is low; profit comes from stacking opponent when you flop a set (deep stacks help).
explanations.B: Pocket fives vs UTG open is well below 50%.
explanations.C: Missing the set means often folding postflop.
explanations.D: Direct pot odds rarely justify set mines without implied odds.

remediation.question: Set mining is profitable mainly when:
options: Stacks are deep enough to win big on a set

explanations.A: Correct — shallow stacks kill implied odds.
explanations.D: Overpairs still exist; implied odds matter when you flop.

remediation.question: Approximate flop set rate with a pocket pair:

explanations.D: 35% is closer to a flush draw by river.
